title: Does store music influence shoppers without them noticing? — Daniel Fox
meta_description: In the famous wine study, 86 percent of shoppers swore the music didn't affect them while it drove a 5-to-1 sales swing. I coded ten landmark field experiments: exactly two ever asked the shoppers, and both found the same gap.

schema.headline: Does store music influence shoppers without them noticing?
schema.description: How music shapes shopper behavior below conscious awareness, through priming, emotion, and time perception. Includes an original coding of the field's landmark experiments: across ten field studies from 1966 to 2019, Fox found exactly two that asked shoppers about the music afterward, and both found behavior moving while awareness stayed flat.

faq.q: Do shoppers know when store music is influencing them?
faq.a: Mostly no, in the two field experiments that measured it. In the 1999 wine study, only 1 of 44 questioned buyers spontaneously named the music as a reason for their choice, and only 6 of 44 said yes when asked directly, while the music drove a five-to-one swing in what sold. In Milliman's 1982 supermarket study, exit interviews found shoppers recalled music at statistically identical rates whether slow, fast, or no music was playing.

faq.q: How many store-music studies have actually asked shoppers about the music?
faq.a: Daniel Fox coded ten landmark field experiments from 1966 to 2019, in supermarkets, restaurants, bars, and a wine cellar, and found exactly two that asked shoppers about the music afterward: Milliman 1982 and North 1999. Both found the same gap between measured behavior and what shoppers could report.

faq.q: Does this mean store music is subliminal manipulation?
faq.a: The studies don't support that word. Milliman wrote that shoppers were "something less than totally conscious" of the music rather than completely unaware, and the wine-study authors flag that some customers may simply not have wanted to admit the influence. The defensible claim is the gap: behavior moved measurably while self-reported awareness stayed near zero.
